title: Doping-Induced Brightening of Dark Excitons and Trions in a WSe$_2$ Monolayer
authors: Grzegorz Krasucki, Artur O. Slobodeniuk, Kacper Walczyk, Katarzyna Olkowska-Pucko, Kenji Watanabe, Takashi Taniguchi, Adam Babiński, Maciej R. Molas
abstract: Optically dark excitonic states play a critical role in the valleytronic, electronic, and optical properties of monolayer semiconducting transition metal dichalcogenides. Here, we investigate how electrostatic doping affects the in-plane magnetic-field-induced activation of dark excitonic complexes in a gated WSe$_2$ monolayer. By continuously tuning the carrier density via gate voltage, we access $n$-type, charge-neutral, and $p$-type regimes and track the corresponding brightening dynamics. We find that the brightening rates of the dark negative trion ($T^{D-}$), dark neutral exciton ($X^{D}$), and dark positive trion ($T^{D+}$) exhibit a strong and nontrivial dependence on doping. In particular, the pronounced asymmetry in the brightening behaviour of the neutral $X^{D}$ complex and the charged $T^{D-}$ and $T^{D+}$ trions reveals distinct underlying carrier interactions, which we describe using a rate-equation model for their steady-state populations. These findings highlight the key role of dark excitonic complexes in governing the optical response and carrier dynamics of doped S-TMD monolayers.
summary: This paper explores how changing the number of electrons or holes in a WSe2 monolayer affects the way dark excitons and trions become visible under a magnetic field. By using a gate voltage, the authors tune the material through n-type, neutral, and p-type regimes. They discovered that the way these dark states 'brighten' is highly asymmetric depending on the doping. This work provides a deeper understanding of how charge carriers influence the optical properties of transition metal dichalcogenides.
main_problem: The study investigates how electrostatic doping affects the in-plane magnetic-field-induced brightening (optical activation) of dark excitons and dark trions in monolayer WSe2.
main_result: The researchers found a strong, non-trivial dependence of brightening rates on carrier density, revealing a pronounced asymmetry between the neutral dark exciton and charged dark trions.
method: Low-temperature micro-photoluminescence spectroscopy was performed on a gated WSe2 heterostructure under in-plane magnetic fields up to 16 T, with data analyzed using a steady-state rate-equation model.
model_or_system: The physical system is a monolayer WSe2 (S-TMD) heterostructure (hBN/WSe2/hBN/graphene) where carrier density is tuned via gate voltage through n-type, neutral, and p-type regimes.
key_observables: Integrated PL intensities of bright and dark excitonic complexes (X_B, X_D, T_D-, T_D+), brightening factors (alpha), and carrier densities.
important_parameters: In-plane magnetic field (up to 16 T), gate voltage, carrier density (10^10 to 10^12 cm^-2), and temperature (10 K).
main_assumptions: The rate-equation model assumes thermal dissociation is negligible when energy splitting is much larger than kBT and that the brightening factor is proportional to the dark complex population and bright analogue intensity.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the device schematic; Figure S1 provides a PL map of various complexes across different doping regimes; Figure S2 displays magneto-PL maps and plots of integrated intensities vs. magnetic field with quadratic fits.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of dark exciton activation, describes the experimental device and spectroscopic setup, presents the results of doping-dependent brightening, provides a rate-equation model to explain the observed asymmetry, and concludes with the implications for TMD carrier dynamics.
why_interesting: The study explores the controlled optical activation of spin-forbidden states and the complex many-body dynamics of excitonic complexes, which is relevant to understanding light-matter interactions and carrier recombination in two-dimensional systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic illustration of the investigated het- erostructure. The gated device consists of hBN/WSe2 ML/hBN/graphene, with Pt contacts to the graphene layer and the WSe2 ML. Applying an external voltage enables con- trol of the carrier density in the WSe2 ML. (b-d) Schematic diagrams of (b) the dark exciton (XD), (c) the dark negative trion (T D−), and (d) the dark positive trion (T D+).

FIG. 2. False-colour map of the low-temperature (T = 10 K) PL of a WSe2 ML versus gate voltage, measured in an in-plane magnetic field of 16 T. The n-type, charge-neutral, and p-type regimes are indicated, with the neutrality point marked. The observed excitonic complexes, including bright (XB, T +, T − S , and T − T ), dark (XD, T D−, and T D+), and phonon replicas of dark complexes (T D+ p , T D− p , I+ p , and I− p ), are labeled. Their origin is discussed in the text.

FIG. 3. (a)-(c) False-colour maps of low-temperature (T = 10 K) PL of a WSe2 ML versus in-plane magnetic field for three representative gate voltages: (a) n-doped regime, (b) neutrality point, and (c) p-doped regime. The colour-coded intensities in panels (a)-(c) are normalised to the maximum intensity in each panel for clarity. (d)-(f) Comparison of the corresponding PL spectra in the three regimes measured at zero magnetic field (blue curves) and at B = 16 T (red curves). The intensities in panels (d)–(f) correspond to the absolute values measured in the experiment. The intensity in panel (d) is therefore scaled by a factor of 0.5.
FIG. 5. Extracted brightening factors (α) of the XD, T D, T D−, and T D+ transitions as a function of the gate voltage. The vertical dashed line marks the charge neutrality point.

FIG. 4. Magnetic-field dependence of the integrated intensi- ties of dark excitonic complexes extracted for three represen- tative gate voltages: (a) n-doped regime, (b) the neutrality point, and (c) p-doped regime. Coloured curves represent fits to the function ID = I0 + αB2.
